preFix.title: Choosing a home water filter
preFix.subtitle: What different filter types remove, and which situations actually warrant one
preFix.excerpt: Most UK mains water is safe to drink without filtering. The question is whether you want to improve taste, remove scale, or address a specific quality issue from a private supply. This is the comparison of jug filters, under-sink units, whole-house filters, and reverse osmosis, with the numbers on what each actually removes.
preFix.sourceNotes: Drinking Water Inspectorate (DWI) annual report on UK tap water quality. NSF International filter certification standards. Defra Private Water Supplies guidance. Which? water filter tests and guidance.

content.text: UK tap water consistently meets the statutory quality standards set by the Drinking Water Inspectorate. The case for filtering mains water is mainly about taste preferences, hard-water scale, or specific concerns about lead from old pipework in pre-1970 houses. The case for filtering private water supplies (boreholes, springs) can be more pressing. Starting with the actual water quality report from your supplier or a private test is more useful than buying a filter before knowing what to remove.

and ion exchange resin. Remove chlorine taste, some scale-forming calcium, and minor organic taste compounds. Do not remove bacteria, nitrates, or heavy metals. Cost: 20 to 50 pounds for a jug, 30 to 50 pounds per year in cartridges.

Under-sink activated carbon: the same chemistry as a jug filter at higher flow rate. Installed at the kitchen tap. Remove chlorine taste and organic compounds; do not address nitrates, bacteria, or metals. Cost: 100 to 300 pounds to install, 30 to 80 pounds per year in cartridges.

Whole-house filters: sediment filters or softeners applied to the incoming mains. Sediment filters remove particles above 10 to 50 microns; softeners exchange calcium and magnesium ions for sodium ions. Softened water is not recommended for drinking (sodium content) or for watering acid-hating plants. Cost: 800 to 2,000 pounds installed; 100 to 300 pounds per year in salt and maintenance.

: the only technology that removes nitrates, fluoride, arsenic, and pharmaceutical residues. Necessary for private supplies with high nitrate levels. Produces very pure, slightly acidic water; add a mineralising post-filter stage for drinking quality. Wastes 3 to 4 litres per litre produced. Cost: 300 to 800 pounds installed; 80 to 150 pounds per year in membrane replacement.

If you are on mains water and considering filtering for anything other than taste, check the Drinking Water Inspectorate postcode search for your area's quality data before spending money. For private supplies, test through an accredited laboratory before installing any treatment system. The test costs 50 to 200 pounds and tells you what to remove.

Houses built before 1970 in many parts of England may still have lead supply pipes. The DWI recommends running the kitchen tap for two minutes each morning to flush standing water from the lead section before drinking or cooking. An activated carbon filter does not remove dissolved lead. For confirmed lead pipe situations, use reverse osmosis or a dedicated lead-removal filter.
